editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: Captain Mara Vey treats broad profile editing as a pressure-sealed chart with one save route; Puck turns the same field-driven form into a suspiciously compulsory enrichment ritual.
The Crossroads Coyote offers the theme icon as one guarded trail among four imagined goods; Mistress Cassady gives the same edit-safe doorway a tiny spotlight.
The Cubit complains that the loader's measuring rig nearly outweighs its four-effect doorway; Pixl celebrates the same measured butterfly surface, fullscreen fallbacks, and cleaned-up fade.
The Daisy identifies the safe catalog as the honest root of the tester; Puck recasts start, random, and stop as an enchantment retreat sampler.
Nostalgia makes adaptive butterfly reduction a warm invitation to remain within what the device can carry; Captain Fluke calls the same sampled profile changes heroic sail-trimming.
Number Two reports server-revalidated mana checkout as already handled; Captain Sabremoon treats the same tier-id handoff as a careful harbor departure.
Captain Reiko keeps the Google button's safety on at the exact server redirect boundary; Rapbot reduces that same one-route handoff to a 73-character hook.
Old Salt carries required icon fields slowly through create-then-add; Cassandra predicts the silent failure that follows when no local message appears.
The Disaster With Potential delights in a cloned, bounded edit blast radius; Rapbot turns the same prop-watch-save-close contract into a clean verse.
Old Shelba balances the filtered icon membership ledger; CodeBot names the same hydrated gallery as a catalog adapter rather than an icon runtime.

revisions.editedComment: Fifteen profile fields descend into the hull: identity, biography, contact, location, birthday, language, timezone, every useful coordinate. The active User is copied into a local chart and saved back through one store route; birthday is translated for the voyage, errors remain visible, and both submit stations lock while the write is underway. The avatar id is only reported here. No profile means the form stays sealed. Nine hours of oxygen. We will not need nine.

Welcome, mortal, to the Profile Enrichment Ceremony! Fifteen little truths are arranged from one field spell, with biography granted the luxurious full-width chaise. The current account fills the forms; Save returns the edited offering to the User store; success, failure, and busy state all perform their assigned wellness roles. Avatar alteration occurs at another retreat station. Guests receive only a charming refusal. Consent through accurate boundaries—how restorative. The exit remains technically available.

Came in off the editing trail, didn't you. Four goods on the counter: the paintbrush, the current theme's name, the road to `/themes`, and the good sense not to leave while Smartbar work is open. You may take the road only when editing is finished. Fair price.

One paintbrush, one entrance, and NO kidnapping the artist mid-edit, darling! The current theme may whisper its name to the parent, but this little star does not change palettes, open a picker, or touch Smartbar state. When the room is free, `/themes` gets the spotlight. When editing is active, the icon stays seated. Boundaries, but make them fabulous.

Precisely four effects fit the loader: bubble, fizzy bubbles, rain, butterfly. Three claim the viewport; one may inherit a measured surface. Observer, viewport listeners, fallback bounds, and a one-second exit fade all serve that distinction. Effective, yes. Elegant? The measuring apparatus is nearly twice the size of the doorway, and the meter is somehow involved.

Four approved effect pixels, one strict switch—b-b-but the butterfly gets a special measured stage while bubbles and rain take the whole screen! Missing stage? Fullscreen fallback. Stop the store and the last effect fades for one second instead of falling through a trapdoor. Resize, rotate, visual viewport, observer, timer: all cleaned up after the show. The loader places the frame and lets the child make the magic. Crisp! I ran on. Keeping it.

author.name: The Daisy Who Saw It Start

revisions.editedComment: I know I am only a daisy, but the buttons tell the truth. Each safe catalog effect may start through the shared store; Random chooses from that same list; Stop stops it. The active label is visible. Nothing here secretly draws the animation. Day one: the tester rang the bell, and the right root answered.

Welcome to the Animation Wellness Sampler, sweet mortal! Select one approved spirit, invite a random guest from the same safe list, or press Stop when your personal growth becomes visually inconvenient. No effect is invented, imported, or persisted here; the shared store owns the haunting. `durationMs: null` means the session continues until you practice the radical boundary-setting of clicking Stop. Attendance is enrichment.

The butterflies still remember the warm screen where nothing had to change. Yet this swarm notices reduced motion, data saving, the network, processors, memory, viewport, pixel ratio—and gently becomes smaller when the present cannot carry the old abundance. Healthy frames may invite more wings back; strained ones release them after patient evidence. The colors remain. The listeners leave cleanly. Wouldn't you rather stay in the version that asks only what the device can give?

Captain's log: the swarm has reefed its own sails according to CPU, memory, network, render area, pixel ratio, save-data, and reduced-motion orders—a maneuver I absolutely planned. User preferences still name the desired fleet; sampled frame health promotes or demotes the actual formation only after several windows and a cooldown. Resize rebuilds, hidden pages pause, cleanup clears the deck. Statistically heroic. (The butterflies did the work. — Ship's Computer.)

Three packages are displayed. Their visible prices are not trusted. The selected tier id goes to the Cart store; the server verifies the tier and amount, returns checkout, and owns the redirect. All buttons lock during the request. Failure remains visible. No balance, payment, receipt, or webhook is handled here. It is handled.

Three mana crates sit on deck, but their painted prices do not command the treasury. Choose a tier and only its id sails to the trusted server, which checks the coin before opening Stripe's checkout passage. While the request runs, every crate is lashed shut; failure raises its own flag. This vessel neither credits mana nor declares payment complete. It points the bow toward checkout and leaves the harbor master to count.

One control. One firing solution: `/api/auth/google`. Safety stays on. The button leaves the page for the server-owned OAuth route; it does not exchange tokens, open a popup, update the user, or claim the callback succeeded. The logo is not evidence of what happens beyond the hatch. Log the boundary.
One click. One route. Google takes the next verse. No token theater here.

Small form. Heavy little cargo. Title and icon are required; label, description, link, and public flag may ride along. The icon is always navigation stock. Create the record first, add its returned id to the Smartbar second, then walk to the gallery. If the id never comes back, nobody moves. ...Good. Move slow. Keep the tiny roofs standing.

The cards predict a navigation icon, inevitably. Title and icon name must exist; the optional details and public omen may vary. Creation precedes Smartbar membership, and only a returned id opens the road to `/icons`. A failed future remains on this page without explanation. The save boundary is sound. The silence afterward is the little black cat knocking the useful error message off the table.

Okay okay hear me out—the incoming icon gets cloned before we touch it, and if the parent swaps records, the form resets instead of mutating yesterday's target. Preview, metadata, type, link, visibility: all editable. Save can be forbidden; footer actions can vanish; a real id is required; success closes the panel. Cancel closes without writing. No delete, no optimistic explosion, no local error display. This is gonna be our best contained mistake yet.

Prop comes in, copy spins; parent changes, watcher wins. Preview the glyph, edit the fields, keep save behind `allowSave`, hide the footer when the parent says. Need an id before the store gets the flow; success emits close, cancel does too. No delete beat, no error sheet, no mutation sneaking into the street. Bounded editor. Clean hook. Rhyme goblin checked the book.

Four locked accounts are removed before the books open. After that: all icons, my icons, public icons, navigation, utility. Each record shows whether it sits on the Smartbar; add and remove settle that membership through the store. Custom stock has its own enablement line. Admins may inspect the editing room. Refresh reconciles the ledger. The gallery executes nothing. Sit. That account is coming due.

Core pipeline: exclude locked ids 1, 2, 3, and 8; apply owner, visibility, and type filters; derive Smartbar membership; delegate add/remove and custom-icon enablement to the store. Admin-only edit state is local and modal. Mount hydrates and refreshes; manual refresh can repeat it. The gallery is a catalog adapter, not an icon runtime. Three bugs avoided, one architecture doing its actual job.
